skill.name: browser-use
skill.description: Browser automation skill that uses Playwright MCP to navigate pages, inspect browser state, fill forms, capture screenshots, and extract web content.
skill.summary: Automate browser navigation, testing, screenshots, forms, and web data extraction through Playwright MCP.
skill.author: 92Bilal26

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many hits, but most markdown hits are false positives from command examples, tool names, or JSON Schema "$schema" text. The confirmed risks are legitimate but elevated browser automation capabilities: starting external processes, making local MCP HTTP calls, navigating arbitrary sites, uploading files, saving screenshots or snapshots, and executing page JavaScript through Playwright MCP.

medium_findings.title: User-Supplied Stdio Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The bundled MCP client accepts a --stdio command and launches it with subprocess.Popen using shell=True. This is useful for local MCP servers, but it can execute arbitrary shell commands if an unsafe command string is supplied.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command string is passed directly to Popen with shell=True. The behavior is explicit MCP functionality, so risk depends on who controls the CLI arguments.

medium_findings.title: Dynamic Browser JavaScript and Playwright Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill exposes browser_evaluate and browser_run_code workflows that execute caller-provided JavaScript or Playwright code in the active browser page. This is expected for automation, but it can read page content, interact with authenticated sessions, or perform unwanted actions on sites the browser can access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly defines JavaScript and Playwright code execution parameters. This is not hidden or malicious, but it is a powerful capability.

medium_findings.title: Browser Automation Can Access Network and Local Files
medium_findings.description: The skill can navigate to arbitrary URLs, upload absolute local file paths, and save screenshots or snapshots. These capabilities are normal for browser automation, but they require user awareness because page data or local files can be exposed through browser actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The tool schemas explicitly document URL navigation, absolute file uploads, and optional file output. The risk is contextual because these are expected features for this skill.

low_findings.title: Static Markdown Pattern False Positives
low_findings.description: Many static hits in references/playwright-tools.md and SKILL.md are markdown code fences, JSON Schema "$schema" fields, command examples, or generated tool names. I did not find evidence that these markdown hits are hidden code execution or weak cryptography in the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation examples and schema metadata, not executable cryptographic code. The static scanner appears to match markdown punctuation and literal schema URLs.

low_findings.title: PID File Management in Temporary Directory
low_findings.description: The lifecycle scripts store a Playwright MCP PID file under /tmp and later read or remove it. This is a common local helper pattern, but stale or tampered PID files could affect the wrong process if used in a shared environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The temporary PID file behavior is clear. The impact is limited for local use, but shared hosts can create process-control edge cases.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.Popen with shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: The MCP stdio transport launches the provided command through the shell. Use only trusted stdio MCP commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly shows shell=True with a command string. It is dangerous if untrusted input reaches the --stdio argument.

dangerous_patterns.title: Playwright Code Execution Tooling
dangerous_patterns.description: The tool documentation includes browser_run_code, which runs caller-provided Playwright code against the page.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tool schema directly describes execution of a JavaScript function with page access. This is intentional but high-impact functionality.

dangerous_patterns.title: File Upload by Absolute Path
dangerous_patterns.description: The browser_file_upload tool accepts absolute local file paths. Users must avoid uploading sensitive files to untrusted pages.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The schema explicitly supports absolute file paths. Exposure depends on the target site and user prompt.

content.value_statement: Manual browser tasks slow down testing, research, and data collection. This skill connects Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to Playwright MCP for repeatable browser actions.
actual_capabilities: Start and stop a local Playwright MCP server for browser sessions.
Navigate to URLs, go back, manage tabs, and wait for page conditions.
Capture accessibility snapshots, screenshots, console messages, and network requests.
Click, type, fill forms, select options, handle dialogs, hover, drag, and press keys.
Run page JavaScript or multi-step Playwright code through MCP tools.
Upload files by path and save screenshots or snapshots when requested.
limitations: Requires Node.js, npx, Python 3, and Playwright MCP availability in the local environment.
Browser actions can affect real websites and authenticated sessions.
Element references can become stale after navigation or page updates.
Does not add site-specific bypasses for CAPTCHAs, paywalls, or access controls.

use_cases.title: Test Web Interfaces
use_cases.description: Exercise forms, buttons, navigation flows, and screenshots during manual or exploratory UI testing.

use_cases.title: Collect Public Web Data
use_cases.description: Navigate public pages, inspect page state, and extract visible information into a concise summary.

use_cases.title: Automate Repetitive Browser Tasks
use_cases.description: Repeat known browser workflows such as filling internal forms, checking dashboards, and capturing evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Open a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open https://example.com, take a page snapshot, and summarize the visible headings and links.

prompt_templates.title: Fill a Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Navigate to the form page, identify each field with a snapshot, fill the values I provide, and stop before final submission.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Test Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Run through this user flow, record console errors, capture network failures, and save a screenshot of the final state.

prompt_templates.title: Automate a Multi-Step Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Playwright MCP to complete this multi-step browser workflow, wait for each state change, and report every action and result.

output_examples.input: Open a product page and summarize the visible pricing details.
output_examples.output: The page opened successfully. The visible plan names, prices, billing terms, and primary call-to-action labels were summarized.

output_examples.input: Check whether the sign-up form accepts invalid email input.
output_examples.output: The form rejected the invalid email address and displayed a validation message near the email field. A screenshot was captured.

output_examples.input: Inspect browser console and network activity after loading the dashboard.
output: Two warning-level console messages were found.
No failed document requests were observed.

best_practices: Use snapshots before clicks or typing so element references match the current page.
Confirm before submitting forms, uploading files, or acting inside authenticated accounts.
Stop the Playwright MCP server when browser work is complete.
anti_patterns: Do not run browser automation against websites where you lack permission.
Do not upload sensitive local files to untrusted pages.
Do not pass untrusted command strings to the stdio MCP client option.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates browser navigation, form input, clicks, screenshots, snapshots, network checks, and Playwright MCP actions.

faq.question: Does it require a local server?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill starts a local Playwright MCP server and sends MCP requests to it.

faq.question: Can it run JavaScript on a page?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents browser_evaluate and browser_run_code for page JavaScript and Playwright code execution.

faq.question: Can it work with logged-in websites?
faq.answer: Yes, when the browser session has access. Users should approve actions that affect accounts or private data.

faq.question: Can it upload files?
faq.answer: Yes. The Playwright MCP file upload tool accepts local file paths, so users should choose files carefully.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with warnings. The capabilities are legitimate, but they can affect web sessions, files, and local processes.
